title: Abjuration
summary: Abjuration spells are protective in nature, creating barriers, negating harmful effects, harming trespassers, or banishing creatures to other planes of existence.
content:
## Abjuration

**Focus:** Protection, warding, banishment, negation

Abjuration spells are the magic of defense. They create protective barriers, negate harmful effects, harm trespassers, and banish creatures to other planes. If evocation is the sword, abjuration is the shield. An abjurer doesn't just survive -- they make sure *everyone* survives.

### How It Works Thematically

Abjuration magic draws on the fundamental forces that hold reality together and uses them to create boundaries. An abjuration spell says "this far and no further." It defines a space, a person, or a concept as protected, and then enforces that protection with magical energy. The magic is inherently *reactive* -- it exists to counter, block, and deny.

### Notable / Iconic Spells

- **Shield** (1st level) -- The quintessential abjuration; +5 AC as a reaction. Every wizard's best friend.
- **Counterspell** (3rd level) -- Negate another caster's spell as a reaction. Defines high-level magical combat.
- **Dispel Magic** (3rd level) -- Strip away magical effects. Essential utility.
- **Banishment** (4th level) -- Send a creature to another plane. Fight-ending if they fail the save.
- **Globe of Invulnerability** (6th level) -- A sphere where no spell of 5th level or lower can penetrate.
- **Antimagic Field** (8th level) -- Suppress all magic in a 10-foot sphere. The nuclear option.
- **Absorb Elements** (1st level) -- Halve elemental damage and add it to your next attack.
- **Mage Armor** (1st level) -- AC 13 + Dex for unarmored characters.
- **Protection from Evil and Good** (1st level) -- Broad protection against six creature types.

### Wizard Subclass: School of Abjuration

The **School of Abjuration** wizard gains:

- **Abjuration Savant** -- Copy abjuration spells into your spellbook at half cost and time
- **Arcane Ward** -- A protective magical barrier that absorbs damage, recharging when you cast abjuration spells
- **Projected Ward** -- Use your Arcane Ward to protect allies within 30 feet
- **Improved Abjuration** -- Add proficiency bonus to ability checks for abjuration spells (boosting Counterspell and Dispel Magic)
- **Spell Resistance** -- Advantage on saves against spells, and resistance to spell damage

The Abjuration wizard is arguably the tankiest wizard subclass, able to absorb significant damage through their Arcane Ward while maintaining concentration on key spells.

### Common Use Cases

- **Protecting the party** -- Shield, Mage Armor, Protection from Evil and Good, Warding Bond
- **Anti-magic combat** -- Counterspell, Dispel Magic, Antimagic Field
- **Dealing with outsiders** -- Banishment, Protection from Evil and Good, Magic Circle, Planar Binding
- **Securing locations** -- Glyph of Warding, Guards and Wards, Forbiddance, Private Sanctum
- **Elemental defense** -- Absorb Elements, Protection from Energy, Resistance

### How to Think About This School as a Player

Abjuration is the school of **"no."** Enemy casts a spell? Counterspell. Enemy deals damage? Shield. Enemy tries to enter your space? Magic Circle. Enemy exists on this plane? Banishment.

It's reactive and defensive, which means it's not flashy -- but it's *incredibly* powerful. A well-played abjurer controls the battlefield by removing threats rather than creating explosions. If you enjoy being the person who keeps the party alive, who shuts down enemy casters, and who always has an answer ready, abjuration is your school.

**Pro tip:** Counterspell and Dispel Magic are the two most important spells in high-level D&D. Both are abjuration. That alone makes this school essential.

title: Conjuration
summary: Conjuration spells transport creatures and objects, summon allies, create things from nothing, and open portals to other planes of existence.
content:
## Conjuration

**Focus:** Summoning, teleportation, creation

Conjuration spells bring things into being or move them from one place to another. A conjurer can summon a demon from the Abyss, teleport across the world, create food from thin air, or open a portal to another plane. If you want to be somewhere else, bring something here, or create something from nothing, conjuration is your school.

### How It Works Thematically

Conjuration magic manipulates the connections between places, planes, and the raw fabric of creation. Summoning spells reach across planar boundaries to pull creatures to the caster. Teleportation spells fold space so that two distant points briefly touch. Creation spells draw on the raw potential of magic itself to manifest physical objects. The school asks: "Why walk when you can teleport? Why fight alone when you can summon an army?"

### Notable / Iconic Spells

- **Misty Step** (2nd level) -- Bonus action teleport 30 feet. One of the best spells in the game.
- **Dimension Door** (4th level) -- Teleport 500 feet and bring a friend.
- **Teleportation Circle** (5th level) -- Create a permanent teleportation network.
- **Conjure Animals** (3rd level) -- Summon beasts to overwhelm enemies with action economy.
- **Conjure Elemental** (5th level) -- Summon a powerful elemental (careful with concentration!).
- **Web** (2nd level) -- Create difficult terrain that restrains creatures. Incredible control spell.
- **Cloudkill** (5th level) -- Toxic fog that moves and deals damage.
- **Gate** (9th level) -- Open a portal to another plane; can summon specific creatures.
- **Wish** (9th level) -- The most powerful spell in the game (partly conjuration).

### Wizard Subclass: School of Conjuration

The **School of Conjuration** wizard gains:

- **Conjuration Savant** -- Copy conjuration spells at half cost and time
- **Minor Conjuration** -- Create a nonmagical object up to 3 feet on a side as an action; lasts 1 hour
- **Benign Transposition** -- Teleport 30 feet or swap places with a willing ally; recharges on spell cast
- **Focused Conjuration** -- Can't lose concentration on conjuration spells from taking damage
- **Durable Summons** -- Summoned creatures gain 30 temporary HP

The Conjuration wizard excels at utility and summoning. Focused Conjuration is extremely powerful, ensuring your summoned allies stay on the field even when you take hits.

### Common Use Cases

- **Battlefield control** -- Web, Grease, Fog Cloud, Sleet Storm, Cloudkill
- **Summoning allies** -- Conjure Animals, Conjure Elemental, Conjure Fey, Summon spells
- **Travel** -- Misty Step, Dimension Door, Teleport, Teleportation Circle, Plane Shift, Gate
- **Escape** -- Misty Step, Dimension Door, Thunder Step
- **Creation and utility** -- Create Food and Water, Unseen Servant, Magnificent Mansion

### How to Think About This School as a Player

Conjuration is the school of **"more."** More allies on the field (summoning), more options for positioning (teleportation), more resources (creation). It's also the school of **"elsewhere"** -- moving yourself, your allies, or your enemies to where they need (or don't want) to be.

Summoning spells are powerful because they give you extra actions every round. Teleportation spells are powerful because positioning is everything in combat. Web is powerful because restraining enemies is one of the best conditions in the game.

**Pro tip:** Misty Step + Dimension Door are the two spells that will save your life more than any others in the game. Both are conjuration.

title: Divination
summary: Divination spells reveal information, whether in the form of secrets long forgotten, glimpses of the future, hidden locations, or the truth behind illusions.
content:
## Divination

**Focus:** Knowledge, foresight, detection, communication

Divination spells reveal information. They let you see the invisible, know the unknowable, glimpse the future, and peer across vast distances. A diviner doesn't need to be the strongest or the fastest -- they just need to know more than everyone else.

### How It Works Thematically

Divination magic accesses the flow of information that permeates the multiverse. Every event sends ripples through time and space, and divination magic reads those ripples. Some divination spells look backward (Legend Lore), some look forward (Divination, Foresight), and some look sideways (Detect Magic, See Invisibility). The school is about *awareness* -- understanding the situation better than anyone else.

### Notable / Iconic Spells

- **Detect Magic** (1st level) -- The single most-used spell in D&D. See magical auras within 30 feet.
- **Identify** (1st level) -- Learn the properties of a magic item. Can also detect spells on creatures.
- **See Invisibility** (2nd level) -- See invisible creatures and into the Ethereal Plane.
- **Clairvoyance** (3rd level) -- Place a sensor at a known location and see or hear through it.
- **Divination** (4th level) -- Ask your god a question and receive a short answer.
- **Scrying** (5th level) -- Spy on a creature anywhere in the world.
- **True Seeing** (6th level) -- See through all illusions, shapeshifting, and into the Ethereal Plane.
- **Foresight** (9th level) -- 8 hours of advantage on everything and enemies have disadvantage against you. One of the best 9th-level spells.
- **Find Traps** (2nd level) -- Often considered one of the worst spells in the game; tells you traps exist but not where.

### Wizard Subclass: School of Divination

The **School of Divination** wizard gains:

- **Divination Savant** -- Copy divination spells at half cost and time
- **Portent** -- Roll 2d20 after a long rest; replace any attack roll, save, or ability check you can see with one of these rolls. *One of the most powerful features in the game.*
- **Expert Divination** -- Casting divination spells of 2nd level or higher recovers a spell slot of lower level
- **The Third Eye** -- Choose darkvision, ethereal sight, read any language, or see invisibility at will
- **Greater Portent** (at 14th level) -- Roll 3d20 for Portent instead of 2

**Portent is gamebreaking.** Rolling a 1 or 2? Force it on the boss's saving throw against your Hold Monster. Rolling a 20? Give it to the rogue for a critical hit. This alone makes Divination one of the strongest wizard subclasses.

### Common Use Cases

- **Investigation and exploration** -- Detect Magic, Identify, Locate Object, Locate Creature, Find the Path
- **Spying and intelligence** -- Scrying, Clairvoyance, Arcane Eye
- **Combat advantage** -- Foresight, True Strike (if your DM allows it), See Invisibility, True Seeing
- **Communication** -- Sending, Telepathy, Rary's Telepathic Bond
- **Narrative power** -- Legend Lore, Commune, Contact Other Plane, Divination

### How to Think About This School as a Player

Divination is the school of **"I already knew that."** It's not about power in combat (though Foresight and Portent are extremely strong); it's about *never being surprised*, never walking into a trap unprepared, and always having the information you need.

In combat, divination is subtle. Outside of combat, it's transformative. A diviner can bypass entire dungeons by scrying ahead, solve mysteries by communing with gods, and locate any creature or object in the world.

**Pro tip:** The Divination wizard's Portent ability is widely considered one of the top 5 class features in the game. It turns the wizard into a fate-manipulator who can guarantee critical moments go the party's way.

title: Enchantment
summary: Enchantment spells affect the minds of others, influencing or controlling their behavior through magical charm, compulsion, or manipulation.
content:
## Enchantment

**Focus:** Mind control, charm, compulsion, behavioral manipulation

Enchantment spells affect the minds of others, influencing or controlling their behavior. They can make enemies into friends, compel creatures to take specific actions, or simply make someone unable to act. Enchantment is the most *morally complicated* school of magic -- you're literally overriding someone's free will.

### How It Works Thematically

Enchantment magic reaches into a creature's mind and adjusts their emotions, perceptions, or will. A charm spell doesn't create affection from nothing -- it amplifies existing social bonds or creates a false sense of trust. A compulsion spell overrides the target's desires with the caster's commands. The magic is insidious because the target often doesn't realize they're being manipulated.

### Notable / Iconic Spells

- **Charm Person** (1st level) -- Make a humanoid regard you as a friendly acquaintance. The classic.
- **Sleep** (1st level) -- Knock creatures unconscious based on HP. Incredibly strong at level 1, falls off later.
- **Hold Person** (2nd level) -- Paralyze a humanoid. Melee attacks auto-crit against paralyzed targets.
- **Suggestion** (2nd level) -- Give a creature a "reasonable" course of action; they follow it for 8 hours.
- **Hypnotic Pattern** (3rd level) -- AoE incapacitation. Can end an encounter instantly.
- **Dominate Person** (5th level) -- Total control over a humanoid for up to 1 minute (concentration).
- **Hold Monster** (5th level) -- Paralyze any creature. Hold Person's big sibling.
- **Mass Suggestion** (6th level) -- Suggestion on up to 12 creatures, no concentration, lasts 24 hours.
- **Power Word Stun** (8th level) -- Instantly stun a creature with 150 HP or fewer. No save.

### Wizard Subclass: School of Enchantment

The **School of Enchantment** wizard gains:

- **Enchantment Savant** -- Copy enchantment spells at half cost and time
- **Hypnotic Gaze** -- As an action, charm a creature within 5 feet (no spell slot required)
- **Instinctive Charm** -- When attacked, redirect the attack to another creature as a reaction (once per rest)
- **Split Enchantment** -- Target two creatures with single-target enchantment spells instead of one
- **Alter Memories** -- Charmed creatures forget they were charmed; erase or modify their memories

Split Enchantment is very strong -- casting Hold Person on two targets simultaneously is devastating. Alter Memories makes enchantment spells much safer to use in social situations.

### Common Use Cases

- **Social encounters** -- Charm Person, Suggestion, Friends, Enthrall
- **Crowd control** -- Hypnotic Pattern, Hold Person/Monster, Sleep, Power Word Stun
- **Domination** -- Dominate Person/Monster/Beast, Geas, Mass Suggestion
- **Single-target shutdown** -- Hold Person/Monster, Command, Tasha's Hideous Laughter
- **Stealth and infiltration** -- Charm Person, Suggestion, Modify Memory

### How to Think About This School as a Player

Enchantment is the school of **"you don't want to fight me."** Instead of blasting enemies with fireballs, you make them stop fighting. Instead of sneaking past guards, you make the guards let you in. Instead of negotiating for hours, you suggest a favorable outcome.

The moral dimension is real and worth engaging with: your character is controlling minds. Some tables treat this as deeply evil; others treat it as a tool like any other. Discuss with your group.

Mechanically, enchantment is one of the most powerful schools. Hold Person + melee allies = automatic critical hits. Hypnotic Pattern can end encounters before they begin. Suggestion is limited only by your creativity.

**Pro tip:** Hold Person + rogue ally is one of the most devastating combos in the game. Paralyzed targets grant advantage (enabling Sneak Attack) and melee hits are automatic crits (doubling Sneak Attack dice).

metadata_json.focus: Mind control, charm, compulsion, behavioral manipulation

title: Evocation
summary: Evocation spells manipulate magical energy to produce a desired effect -- most commonly dealing damage through fire, lightning, cold, and other elemental forces.
content:
## Evocation

**Focus:** Elemental damage, energy manipulation, healing

Evocation spells channel magical energy to create powerful effects -- explosions of fire, arcs of lightning, blasts of cold, or waves of healing energy. This is the "blaster" school, the one most people think of when they imagine a wizard. Point, shoot, kaboom.

### How It Works Thematically

Evocation magic draws on raw magical energy and shapes it into a specific form. Unlike conjuration (which summons things) or transmutation (which changes things), evocation *creates* pure energy effects. The caster is essentially a conduit, channeling the raw power of magic and directing it with precision. It's the most direct, least subtle school of magic.

Notably, healing spells are also evocation -- channeling positive energy to mend wounds is thematically similar to channeling fire to cause them.

### Notable / Iconic Spells

- **Fireball** (3rd level) -- *The* iconic D&D spell. 8d6 fire in a 20-foot radius. Every wizard's rite of passage.
- **Lightning Bolt** (3rd level) -- 8d6 lightning in a 100-foot line. Fireball's sibling.
- **Magic Missile** (1st level) -- Guaranteed damage, no attack roll, no save. Always hits (unless Shield).
- **Eldritch Blast** (cantrip) -- The warlock's bread and butter. Multiple beams, force damage.
- **Healing Word** (1st level) -- Bonus action ranged healing. The best low-level healing spell.
- **Cure Wounds** (1st level) -- Touch healing, more HP than Healing Word.
- **Wall of Fire** (4th level) -- Area denial and ongoing damage.
- **Cone of Cold** (5th level) -- 8d8 cold in a 60-foot cone. Massive AoE.
- **Meteor Swarm** (9th level) -- 40d6 damage across four 40-foot-radius spheres. The biggest boom in the game.
- **Sunbeam/Sunburst** (6th/8th level) -- Radiant damage, especially effective against undead.

### Wizard Subclass: School of Evocation

The **School of Evocation** wizard gains:

- **Evocation Savant** -- Copy evocation spells at half cost and time
- **Sculpt Spells** -- When you cast an evocation spell, choose a number of creatures equal to 1 + spell level to automatically succeed on their save and take no damage. *Fireball your allies safely.*
- **Potent Cantrip** -- Cantrips deal half damage on a successful save (instead of no damage)
- **Empowered Evocation** -- Add Intelligence modifier to evocation spell damage
- **Overchannel** -- Maximize the damage of a 5th-level or lower evocation spell (at a cost for repeated use)

**Sculpt Spells is the defining feature.** It solves the biggest problem with AoE damage spells: friendly fire. Drop a Fireball centered on your own fighter and they take zero damage. This transforms evocation from "be careful where you aim" to "aim everywhere."

### Common Use Cases

- **Dealing damage** -- Fireball, Lightning Bolt, Cone of Cold, Meteor Swarm, Disintegrate (wait, that's transmutation!)
- **Area denial** -- Wall of Fire, Wall of Force (actually evocation!), Flaming Sphere
- **Reliable damage** -- Magic Missile, Scorching Ray, Fire Bolt
- **Healing** -- Cure Wounds, Healing Word, Mass Cure Wounds, Heal
- **Lighting** -- Light, Dancing Lights, Daylight, Sunbeam (also combat-relevant)

### How to Think About This School as a Player

Evocation is the school of **"boom."** It's the most straightforward school: you deal damage, lots of it, to lots of creatures at once. It's not subtle, it's not clever, and it doesn't need to be. Sometimes the answer to a problem is a well-placed Fireball.

The school gets a reputation as "simple," but smart evocation play involves:
- Choosing the right damage type for the enemy's vulnerabilities
- Positioning AoE spells to hit the most enemies and fewest allies
- Managing spell slot resources (cantrips vs. leveled spells)
- Using Sculpt Spells to turn "friendly fire" into "only-enemy fire"

**Pro tip:** Fireball is intentionally overtuned for its level -- the designers have confirmed this. It's a 3rd-level spell that deals damage comparable to many 4th-level spells. There's a reason it's iconic.

title: Illusion
summary: Illusion spells deceive the senses and minds of others, creating phantom images, sounds, and sensations that can confuse, misdirect, or terrify.
content:
## Illusion

**Focus:** Deception, misdirection, sensory manipulation, stealth

Illusion spells deceive the senses or minds of others. They cause people to see things that aren't there, miss things that are, hear phantom noises, or remember things that never happened. Illusion magic is limited only by the caster's creativity -- and by whether the DM rewards creative play.

### How It Works Thematically

Illusion magic manipulates perception rather than reality. A fireball creates real fire; a Major Image creates the *appearance* of fire. The difference matters: illusions can't directly deal damage (with some exceptions), but they can cause creatures to *react* as if the threat were real. The school operates on the principle that perception *is* reality -- if everyone believes the bridge is real, it might as well be.

Higher-level illusions blur this line. Mirage Arcane creates illusions that are physically real to the touch. Illusory Reality (the Illusionist's capstone) makes part of an illusion temporarily real.

### Notable / Iconic Spells

- **Minor Illusion** (cantrip) -- Create a sound or a 5-foot image. Unlimited uses. Incredibly versatile.
- **Disguise Self** (1st level) -- Change your appearance for 1 hour. Classic infiltration spell.
- **Invisibility** (2nd level) -- Become invisible until you attack or cast a spell. Stealth perfected.
- **Mirror Image** (2nd level) -- Create 3 duplicates of yourself; attacks might target a duplicate instead.
- **Major Image** (3rd level) -- Create a realistic illusion with sight, sound, smell, and temperature.
- **Greater Invisibility** (4th level) -- Invisibility that *doesn't* end when you attack. Devastating.
- **Phantasmal Force** (2nd level) -- Create an illusion that the target rationalizes as real; deals psychic damage.
- **Hallucinatory Terrain** (4th level) -- Make natural terrain look like other terrain. Ambush enabler.
- **Mirage Arcane** (7th level) -- Transform terrain on a massive scale; the illusion is tactile.
- **Simulacrum** (7th level) -- Create a duplicate of a creature with half its HP. Absurdly powerful.

### Wizard Subclass: School of Illusion

The **School of Illusion** wizard gains:

- **Illusion Savant** -- Copy illusion spells at half cost and time
- **Improved Minor Illusion** -- Minor Illusion creates both sound AND image simultaneously
- **Malleable Illusions** -- Change the nature of an ongoing illusion as an action (reshape, redirect, alter)
- **Illusory Self** -- Create an illusory duplicate to cause an attack to miss (reaction, once per rest)
- **Illusory Reality** -- Make one object in an illusion *real* for 1 minute. A bridge, a wall, a cage -- whatever you imagined, it now exists.

**Illusory Reality is mind-bending.** Cast Major Image of a steel cage around an enemy, then make it real. Create an illusion of a bridge over a chasm, then make it real so the party can cross. The applications are limited only by imagination.

### Common Use Cases

- **Stealth and infiltration** -- Invisibility, Greater Invisibility, Disguise Self, Seeming
- **Misdirection** -- Minor Illusion, Major Image, Silent Image, Programmed Illusion
- **Defense** -- Mirror Image, Blur, Greater Invisibility, Illusory Self
- **Battlefield control** -- Hallucinatory Terrain, Mirage Arcane, Phantasmal Force
- **Social manipulation** -- Disguise Self, Seeming, Modify Memory (enchantment, but thematically adjacent)

### How to Think About This School as a Player

Illusion is the school of **"what if?"** What if there's a wall where there isn't one? What if you look like the king? What if the enemy thinks they're on fire? Illusion is the most creativity-dependent school in the game. A creative player with illusion spells can solve almost any problem. An uncreative player will find them frustrating and weak.

The school's power is also DM-dependent. Some DMs reward creative illusion use generously; others are strict about NPCs' Investigation checks to see through illusions. Talk to your DM about how they handle illusions before committing to this school.

**Pro tip:** Minor Illusion is a *cantrip*. It costs nothing. You can use it an unlimited number of times. A creative player with just this one spell can create distractions, hide behind cover that doesn't exist, fake sounds to lure enemies, and more -- all at no cost.

title: Necromancy
summary: Necromancy spells manipulate the forces of life and death, draining vitality, raising the dead, and creating undead servants.
content:
## Necromancy

**Focus:** Life and death manipulation, undead creation, life drain, resurrection

Necromancy spells manipulate the energies of life and death. Such spells can drain the life force from creatures, raise the dead as undead servants, or even bring the truly dead back to life. Creating undead through necromancy is not a good act, and only evil casters use such spells frequently -- or so the conventional wisdom goes.

### How It Works Thematically

Necromancy taps into the fundamental forces of life and death -- the energy that animates living things and the void that claims them when they die. Positive energy (life) and negative energy (death) are the necromancer's raw materials. Healing spells channel positive energy (though they're classified as evocation in 5e); necromancy deals primarily with negative energy, death, and undeath.

The school occupies a unique moral position in D&D. Some view necromancy as inherently evil -- creating undead traps souls and perverts the natural order. Others see it as a neutral tool: the dead don't need their bodies, and zombie servants don't feel pain.

### Notable / Iconic Spells

- **Toll the Dead** (cantrip) -- Necrotic damage cantrip, deals d12 to already-wounded targets.
- **Inflict Wounds** (1st level) -- 3d10 necrotic damage with a melee touch. Massive single-target damage at low levels.
- **Animate Dead** (3rd level) -- Create a skeleton or zombie servant. The signature necromancer spell.
- **Vampiric Touch** (3rd level) -- Deal necrotic damage and heal for half. Self-sustaining combat spell.
- **Blight** (4th level) -- 8d8 necrotic damage; deals maximum damage to plant creatures.
- **Raise Dead** (5th level) -- Bring a dead creature back to life (within 10 days).
- **Create Undead** (6th level) -- Create more powerful undead (ghouls, ghasts, wights, mummies).
- **Finger of Death** (7th level) -- 7d8+30 necrotic damage; creatures killed by it rise as zombies permanently under your control.
- **True Resurrection** (9th level) -- Restore life to a creature dead for up to 200 years, no body required.

### Wizard Subclass: School of Necromancy

The **School of Necromancy** wizard gains:

- **Necromancy Savant** -- Copy necromancy spells at half cost and time
- **Grim Harvest** -- When you kill a creature with a spell, regain HP (2x spell level for necromancy spells, 3x for non-necromancy)
- **Undead Thralls** -- Animate Dead creates an extra undead; your undead gain extra HP and damage
- **Inured to Undeath** -- Resistance to necrotic damage; HP max can't be reduced
- **Command Undead** -- Take permanent control of an undead creature (Intelligence-based save for smart undead)

The Necromancy wizard is the definitive "army of the dead" build. With Undead Thralls enhancing your minions and Animate Dead allowing daily refresh, you can maintain a growing force of enhanced undead.

### Common Use Cases

- **Damage dealing** -- Toll the Dead, Inflict Wounds, Blight, Finger of Death, Circle of Death
- **Undead army** -- Animate Dead, Create Undead, Finger of Death (permanent zombies)
- **Self-healing** -- Vampiric Touch, Life Transference, Grim Harvest feature
- **Resurrection** -- Revivify (evocation in some versions), Raise Dead, Resurrection, True Resurrection
- **Debuffing** -- Bestow Curse, Blight, Enervation, Contagion
- **Information gathering** -- Speak with Dead, interrogate corpses for information

### How to Think About This School as a Player

Necromancy is the school of **"life and death are mine to command."** It's the most thematically charged school, carrying heavy moral weight in most settings. Playing a necromancer means engaging with questions about the nature of death, the ethics of undead, and where the line is between "using dark magic for good" and "becoming the villain."

Mechanically, necromancy offers two main paths:
1. **The blaster** -- Necrotic damage spells with life-drain kickers
2. **The commander** -- Build an army of undead minions

The minion path is powerful but *logistically demanding*. You need to re-cast Animate Dead every 24 hours to maintain control. Your DM needs to be willing to handle the extra creatures in combat. But when it works, having 8+ skeletons with shortbows firing every round is devastating.

**Pro tip:** Finger of Death creates *permanent* zombies that never need re-animating. In a long campaign, a necromancer with Finger of Death can build an army that grows with every humanoid enemy they kill.

metadata_json.focus: Life and death manipulation, undead creation, life drain, resurrection

title: Transmutation
summary: Transmutation spells change the properties of a creature, object, or environment, altering forms, enhancing abilities, and reshaping the physical world.
content:
## Transmutation

**Focus:** Transformation, enhancement, physical alteration, material change

Transmutation spells change the properties of a creature, object, or environment. They can turn lead into gold, a person into a frog, or a stone wall into a doorway. Transmutation is the school of *change* -- if something is one way, transmutation magic can make it another way.

### How It Works Thematically

Transmutation magic works by altering the fundamental nature of things. Where other schools add energy (evocation), create illusions (illusion), or summon creatures (conjuration), transmutation *changes what already exists*. The philosophy is that nothing is fixed -- everything can be improved, altered, or transformed. Transmuters see the world as raw material waiting to be shaped.

This is the school of the classic alchemist, seeking to perfect base materials into gold or mortal flesh into immortality.

### Notable / Iconic Spells

- **Polymorph** (4th level) -- Transform a creature into a beast. Turn the fighter into a T-Rex. Turn the enemy into a snail.
- **Haste** (3rd level) -- Double speed, +2 AC, extra action. One of the best buff spells in the game.
- **Slow** (3rd level) -- Halve speed, -2 AC, limited actions. Haste's evil twin.
- **Fly** (3rd level) -- Grant flight for 10 minutes. Game-changing mobility.
- **Enlarge/Reduce** (2nd level) -- Make a creature bigger (more damage) or smaller (less threatening).
- **Longstrider** (1st level) -- +10 speed for 1 hour. Simple but effective.
- **Stone Shape** (4th level) -- Reshape stone. Create doors, seal passages, sculpt traps.
- **Disintegrate** (6th level) -- 10d6+40 force damage; reduces to 0 HP = disintegrated entirely.
- **True Polymorph** (9th level) -- Permanently transform any creature or object into any other creature or object.
- **Shapechange** (9th level) -- Become any creature you've seen, retaining your class features.

### Wizard Subclass: School of Transmutation

The **School of Transmutation** wizard gains:

- **Transmutation Savant** -- Copy transmutation spells at half cost and time
- **Minor Alchemy** -- Transform wood, stone, iron, copper, or silver into another of those materials (lasts 1 hour)
- **Transmuter's Stone** -- Create a stone that grants one of: darkvision 60 ft, +10 speed, proficiency in Constitution saves, or resistance to one damage type. Can be given to another creature.
- **Shapechanger** -- Cast Polymorph on yourself without a spell slot to become a beast of CR 1 or lower
- **Master Transmuter** -- Destroy your Transmuter's Stone to: transform one object into another, restore youth, cast Raise Dead, or deal/heal 120 HP

The Transmuter's Stone is versatile, offering flexibility to adapt to different situations. Constitution save proficiency is particularly strong for maintaining concentration spells.

### Common Use Cases

- **Buffing allies** -- Haste, Fly, Enlarge, Longstrider, Alter Self, Enhance Ability
- **Debuffing enemies** -- Slow, Polymorph (into something harmless), Reduce, Flesh to Stone
- **Transformation** -- Polymorph, True Polymorph, Shapechange, Alter Self
- **Utility and problem-solving** -- Stone Shape, Fabricate, Move Earth, Control Water, Passwall
- **Damage** -- Disintegrate, Sunbeam (wait, that's evocation), Catapult (1st level, hurl objects)
- **Environmental manipulation** -- Control Water, Move Earth, Transmute Rock, Reverse Gravity

### How to Think About This School as a Player

Transmutation is the school of **"I can make this better."** It's the most versatile school, offering buffs, debuffs, utility, and combat options. A transmuter doesn't just see a problem -- they see raw materials to reshape into a solution.

The school rewards creative thinking. Stone Shape can bypass locked doors. Polymorph can turn a dangerous enemy into a harmless creature (or turn your fighter into a giant ape). Fabricate can create equipment from raw materials. The transmuter's toolkit is broad and adaptable.

Transmutation also has some of the game's most powerful individual spells. Polymorph, Haste, and True Polymorph are all on many "best spells" lists.

**Pro tip:** Polymorph is one of the most flexible spells in the game. Use it offensively (turn the enemy into a slug), defensively (turn the dying ally into a full-HP T-Rex), or for utility (turn into a spider to squeeze through a crack). It's transmutation's Swiss Army knife.

metadata_json.focus: Transformation, enhancement, physical alteration, material change
